title: The Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## September 20, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost, placing the sermon in Ordinary Time following Pentecost Sunday (which fell on May 31, 2020). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would have been Exodus 16:2-15 (the manna narrative), Psalm 105:1-6, 37-45, Philippians 1:21-30, and Matthew 20:1-16 (the parable of the laborers in the vineyard). The liturgical color would be green, reflecting the extended season of Ordinary Time in the church calendar.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of General Convention 2018 resolutions and ongoing implementation of church-wide initiatives. The Diocese of Colorado was navigating pandemic-altered worship and gathering protocols established in spring 2020. No major diocesan elections or significant canonical controversies were at immediate crisis point in mid-September 2020, though like all Episcopal dioceses, questions about reopening worship spaces safely dominated leadership conversations.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver Public Schools had just returned to remote-only learning on September 8, 2020, after attempting a limited in-person start to the school year, driven by rising COVID-19 case numbers in the metro area. Colorado reported over 40,000 confirmed COVID cases statewide by mid-September. Governor Jared Polis continued managing state response to the pandemic, with economic and health tensions mounting as fall approached. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, was experiencing the typical pressures of an urban core during pandemic conditions: reduced foot traffic, struggling small businesses, and ongoing homelessness challenges.

NATIONAL NEWS

The United States was approximately six weeks from the November 3 presidential election, with the Trump administration and Joe Biden campaign in full intensity. The Supreme Court was about to hear arguments in cases challenging the Affordable Care Act, with oral arguments scheduled for November 2020. The COVID-19 pandemic was accelerating across the United States, with daily death tolls and case counts remaining elevated and widely debated. The Breonna Taylor case dominated national racial justice conversations following the Kentucky grand jury decision (announced September 23, 2020), and the Jacob Blake shooting aftermath was still fresh from August protests.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Nations General Assembly was opening its annual session, with world leaders addressing the pandemic and its global economic consequences. The Belarus protests were in their fourth week following the disputed August 9 presidential election, with ongoing civil unrest and international pressure mounting on the Lukashenko regime. The India-China border tensions remained elevated following clashes in June and July 2020 in the Ladakh region.

CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was characterized by election-year polarization, profound anxiety about the continuing pandemic, and sustained focus on racial justice following high-profile police killings. The Denver Broncos season was beginning (first game September 14, 2020), offering some sports distraction. Americans were experiencing pandemic fatigue at six months in, with "Zoom fatigue" becoming a widespread cultural phenomenon. Masking had become deeply politicized, and debates over school reopening divided many communities. There was a palpable sense of uncertainty about whether normalcy would return and when, with many Americans isolated, grieving, and anxious about both immediate health and broader social stability.
